coredata.dc:description: In this study, a series of carbonized polymer dots were synthesized using Congo red and tryptophan as carbon source by changing their molar ratio through simple one-step method in microwave oven. The prepared carbonized polymer dots could emit red fluorescence. All prepared carbonized polymer dots could inhibit the amyloid aggregation of human lysozyme and the CPDs-3 showed the best inhibition capability. The inhibition ratio of the CPDs-3 reached 95.8% at concentration of 40 μg/mL and the IC50 was 0.41 μg/mL, which may be attributed to the highest content of Congo red on the periphery of the CPDs-3. The CPDs-3 could easily interact with β-sheet structure of human lysozyme through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interaction, which helped to retard the amyloid aggregation. The CPDs-3 also displayed good anti-oxidative property and the highest elimination efficiency for hydroxyl free radical. In addition, the CPDs-3 showed nontoxicity on bEnd.3 cell by CCK-8 assay and could be used in cell imaging.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional carbonized polymer dots: Synthesis and applications in retarding protein amyloid aggregation, capturing free radicals and cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a series of carbonized polymer dots were synthesized using Congo red and tryptophan as carbon source by changing their molar ratio through simple one-step method in microwave oven. The prepared carbonized polymer dots could emit red fluorescence. All prepared carbonized polymer dots could inhibit the amyloid aggregation of human lysozyme and the CPDs-3 showed the best inhibition capability. The inhibition ratio of the CPDs-3 reached 95.8% at concentration of 40 μg/mL and the IC50 was 0.41 μg/mL, which may be attributed to the highest content of Congo red on the periphery of the CPDs-3. The CPDs-3 could easily interact with β-sheet structure of human lysozyme through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interaction, which helped to retard the amyloid aggregation. The CPDs-3 also displayed good anti-oxidative property and the highest elimination efficiency for hydroxyl free radical. In addition, the CPDs-3 showed nontoxicity on bEnd.3 cell by CCK-8 assay and could be used in cell imaging.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The reactive oxygen species (ROS) were generated during metabolic process, and they could be eliminated by the antioxidant system in healthy subjects [1]. However, the excess accumulation of endogenous ROS was present in the organs when the balance was broken between generation and clearance of oxygen species, which was named as oxidative stress [2]. Over expression of ROS could result in aggravated oxidation of the protein leading to accelerated aggregation of proteins into amyloid fibrils [3]. Although the formation mechanism of amyloid fibrils was ambiguous until now, higher level of ROS could also be generated during amyloid aggregation process [4]. Therefore, the effective clearance of ROS would help to retard the formation of amyloid fibrils. The deposition of amyloid fibrils in the organs could cause neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease and type 2 diabetes [5,6]. At present, there was no effective treatment for these diseases [7,8]. Clearance of the free radicals and inhibition of amyloid aggregation would be the most promising methods for treatment of these diseases [9-11].

Human lysozyme (HL) was consisted of 130 amino acid residues and widely existed in the body fluids and tissues. It contained four disulfide bonds and two domains (α-domain and β-domain). The α-domain (residues 1–38 and 86–130) was composed of four α-helical structures and the β-domain (residues 39–85) contained the β-sheet structure. Amyloid aggregates of HL could deposit in various organs (liver, kidney and spleen, ect) and induced the lesion of these organs, which would further cause the non-neurological system amyloidosis [12,13]. Until now, there were no effective treatments for these non-neurological system amyloidosis. Therefore, the study on the HL amyloid aggregation was very important and it was urgent to find the inhibitor to retard the formation of HL amyloid aggregates.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) as one kind of carbon dots, have attracted great attention because of their lower toxicity and good biocompatibility [14-16]. They could be easily prepared using small molecules as precursor and the structure of precursor could be partly retained to CPDs [17-19]. The fluorescence properties of the CPDs were closely related to the precursor. CPDs with red emissive fluorescence could effectively avoid the interference of the autofluorescence from biological molecules in the application of fluorescence imaging and could be obtained using appropriate precursor molecules [20-22]. In addition, a large quantity of end groups on the periphery would greatly benefit CPDs to interact with protein through multiple binding sites compared with the small molecular precursor. The occurrence of the interaction between CPDs and protein would intervene the association between protein molecules, which could potentially retard the protein aggregating into amyloid fibrils. However, it was a great challenge to obtain CPDs with the dual function of simultaneous diagnose and treatment for the amyloid-related diseases.

Congo red (CR) was a dye molecule that was usually utilized to stain the amyloid fibrils because it could easily combine with β-sheet structure of protein [23]. In literature, there were some reports on the synthesis and application of CDs using CR as precursor. For example, the Johnsi group and the Yuan group prepared CR-derived carbon dots used as fluorescence probe for detection of Fe3+[24] and Cu2+[25], respectively. Oxidant doped carbon nanodots were synthesized by the Xu group and were utilized as fluorescence probe for detection of amyloidogenic protein [26]. The Xu group obtained red emissive carbon dots using CR and p-phenylenediamine as carbon source and the prepared carbon dots were utilized to visualize the nucleolar structure of model animals and the drug toxicity in vivo [27]. Yet, to the best of our knowledge, studies on empolying CR-derived carbon dots on protein amyloid aggregation were limited.

In this study, a series of CPDs were synthesized through a simple one-step method by changing the molar ratio of tryptophan and CR in a microwave oven. The prepared CPDs had classic core-shell structure and could emit red fluorescence due to the introduction of CR molecules. The CPDs-3 with more CR structure on their periphery shell showed the best inhibition activity on HL amyloid aggregation and the inhibition ratio could reach 95.8% with 40 μg/mL of the CPDs-3. The CPDs-3 showed nontoxicity on bEnd.3 cell by CCK-8 assay. In addition, the CPDs-3 was also utilized in removing the free radicals and cell imaging. In a word, the prepared CPDs demonstrated the potential multifunctional applications in biochemical fields.

CR was obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tryptophan (Trp), pyrogallol, salicylic acid and 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH•) were supplied by Rhanwn (Shanghai, China). 1,3-Diaminopropane was offered by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HL was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (America). Thioflavin T (ThT) was purchased from J&K Chemicals Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from CAPRICORN (Germany). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and bEnd.3 cell was purchased from iCell (Shanghai, China). Trypsin-EDTA was purchased from Biosharp. Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was purchased from Invigentech (America).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was measured on the (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of CPDs were obtained on an X-ray diffraction Spectrometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) with CuKα as the source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured using a Scientific ESCALAB QXi. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was acquired on FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10, Thermo, USA). The 1H NMR spectra were measured using Oxford AVANCE III 400 MHz spectrometer with tetramethylsilane as the internal standard. UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were performed on UV spectrometer (UV2550 Shimadzu, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan), respectively. Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded in the range of 190-260 nm on the circular dichroism spectrometer (JASCO, J-1500, Japan). The thermogravimetric measurement was conducted on a thermogravimetric analyzer (TA, Q50, America) under a nitrogen atmosphere from room temperature to 800 °C with a heating rate of 20 °C/min. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was implemented on a TAQ2000 instrument from 20 °C to 250 °C at the scanning rate of 10 °C/min under N2 atmosphere.

Six CPDs were prepared using different molar ratio of tryptophan and CR as carbon sources (Table S1). Briefly, tryptophan, CR and 1,3-propylenediamine were added to pure water in a flat flask. The solution was heated in a microwave oven at 640 W for 6 min 30 s. Then pure water was added to the cooling react solution to dissolve the solid. Then, the red solution was dialyzed in 1000 Da dialysis with pure water for 72 h and dialysate was renewed at each 8 h. The solution inside dialysis was freeze-dried to obtain the final CPDs.
The HL sequence in FASTA format was submitted to the AGGRESCAN server online (http://bioinf.uab.es/aggrescan) to calculate the hot spots regions [28].

To prepare amyloid fibrils, HL solution was prepared by dissolving in HCl solution (pH = 1.4) with 140 mM NaCl and the final concentration of HL was 2 mg/mL. Then, the samples were incubated at 60 °C for 5 days. In order to investigate the influence of the CPDs on HL amyloid aggregation, different concentration of the CPDs were added and incubated at the same condition with the control. The growth of amyloid fibrils was monitored by ThT fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elimination ability of CPDs for free radicals
ce:section.ce:para: Three free radicals (DPPH•, •OH and O2 •-) were utilized to investigate the elimination ability of CPDs. For DPPH•, there was a strong absorption peak at 517 nm [29]. When its single electron was captured by the other substance, the absorption peak would be weakened. Briefly, 120 μM DPPH• solution in ethanol was added into the tested solution of specified concentration and the mixed solution was stirred in dark for 0.5 h. Then, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of mixed solution were measured and the elimination efficiency could be calculated basing on the absorbance.

•OH could be produced by mixing FeSO4 (1.2 mM) and H2O2 (3.3 mM) aqueous solution. After 10 min, salicylic acid (1.2 mM) was mixed with the solution. The tested solutions were put into the above mixing solution and stirred in the dark for 0.5 h. The absorbance of salicylic acid at 520 nm would be increased if it was oxidized by •OH [30]. The absorbance was measured and recorded to calculate the elimination efficiency.

O2 •- could be produced during the oxidation process of pyrogallol and the absorption peak at 320 nm was augmented [29]. Therefore, the absorbance with presence of CPDs could be monitored to investigate the elimination ability. Briefly, 100-μL pyrogallol solution (10 mM HCl) was mixed with the tested solution. The mixed solution was diluted to 2 mL with Tris–HCl (50 mM, pH = 8.2) and then the solution was stirred in the dark for 10 min. The absorbance was measured to calculate the elimination efficiency.

The elimination ability of CR and tryptophan for three free radicals was also probed as controls. In this study, the elimination efficiency was calculated according to the following equation [29]:
(1)

100 %
A0 and As were the absorbance of the samples without or with the testing sample such as CPDs, respectively.

The cytotoxicity of CPDs-3 was evaluated using CCK-8 experimental method [30]. The bEnd.3 cells at a density of 8 × 103 were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2. DMEM solution containing FBS (DMEM:FBS=9:1) was utilized as incubation medium. After incubating for 24 h, the DMEM was removed and 100 μL fresh medium with different concentrations of the CPDs was added. The control samples were mixed with 100-μL fresh medium without the CPDs. After incubating for another 24 h under the same condition, the cells were rinsed three times using PBS solution. Then 10% CCK-8 solution (120 μL) in incubation medium was added to each well and the incubation was continued for 2 h. The absorbance of incubation solution at 450 nm was measured using micro-plated reader (TECAN, SPARK 10 M) and recorded. The viability was obtained using the following equation [30]:
(2)

where OD blank , OD control , OD treated were the absorbance of the incubation medium, the control cells and the cells with CPDs, respectively.

The CPDs were dissolved in DMSO and the solution was further diluted with DMEM medium to a final concentration of 100 μg/mL. The bEnd.3 cells were seeded in culture plate at a density of 8 × 104 and cultured in 5% CO2 and DEME medium at 37 °C for 24 h. After the old medium was removed, 1-mL new medium was mixed with the control samples and the prepared CPDs solution was added to the tested samples. All samples were incubated for 4 h at the same condition. All cells were rinsed three times using PBS solution and 1-mL new incubation medium was added to each plate. The samples were observed using confocal microscopy (Olympus, FV3000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of hot spots regions of HL
ce:section.ce:para: The hot spot regions of HL were obtained using AGGRESCAN sever online and the results were shown in Fig. S1. There were four hot spots at 25–33, 54–63, 76–84 and 107–111 fragments, respectively. Comparing the hot spots area graph (Fig. S1B) and the normalized hot spots area graph (Fig. S1C), the hot spot of 107–111 fragment contained the less amino acid residues, but its aggregation propensity was close to that of 23–33 fragment. In the 107–110 fragment, the tryptophan residue (109) showed the highest aggregation propensity (Fig. S1A). If tryptophan was introduced to CPDs, the obtained CPDs might display good inhibition ability on HL aggregation. In addition, CR could interact with β-sheet structure and stain the amyloid fibrils. Introduction of CR into CPDs may endow CPDs the ability to modulate the protein aggregation. Therefore, CR and tryptophan were used as carbon source to prepare CPDs through a simple microwave method (Scheme 1 ).

In the synthesis reaction, 1,3-propylenediamine was added to increase the water solubility of Trp. Six CPDs were prepared by changing the molar ratio (Trp/CR) and the dosage of 1,3-propylenediamine. All reactions were performed at 640 W for 6 min 30 s (Table S1). The structures of six CPDs were similar. Therefore, only the structure of the CPDs-3 was detailed characterized and given in the text. As shown in Fig. 1 A and S2, the morphologies CPDs-3 were well-dispersed quasi-spheres with sizes of 3.75 nm. In HR-TEM (Fig. 1B), there were two lattices spacing. One lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, which was attributed to (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphene and the other lattice spacing was 0.32 nm, which was correlated to (002) inter-layer spacing of graphene [31,32]. This reflected the presence of graphitic carbon-core structure in the CPDs-3.

XRD patterns of the CPDs-3 (Fig. 1C) presented a diffraction peak at 21.4о. The lattice spacing was 0.41 nm calculated according to 2dsinθ = nλ, which was corresponded to the inter-layer spacing of graphene (002) [33,34]. This value was higher than the inter-layer spacing of typical graphene (0.34 nm), which may be caused by the lower crystallinity due to the presence of abundant end groups on the periphery [35].

XPS measurement was utilized to probe the functional groups and the element composition of the CPDs-3. As shown in the full XPS spectra (Fig. 1D), there were C (285.21 eV, 64.22%), N (400.40 eV, 11.58%), O (532.65 eV, 19.21%) and S (169.06 eV, 5.00%) in the CPDs-3. In deconvoluted XPS spectra of C 1 s, four peaks at 284.80 eV, 285.50 eV, 286.29 eV and 287.11 eV (Fig. 1E) were resolved, corresponding to the C-C/C=C, C-S/C-N, C-O and C=O, respectively [27,36-38]. The deconvoluted N 1s spectra were divided into four peaks in Fig. 1F. The peaks at 399.38 eV, 399.95 eV, 400.43 eV, 402.17 eV were attributed to pyridine N, pyrrolic N, C-NH2/ pyrrolic N-H, graphitic N/N-H, respectively [39-42]. For O 1s (Fig. 1G), four peaks at 531.58 eV, 532.29 eV, 533.04 eV and 533.87 eV were originated from C=O, C-O/S=O, S-O and O-H, respectively [27,30,43]. In high resolution spectra of S 2p (Fig. 1H), two peaks of S 2p3/2 (167.81 eV) and S 2p1/2 (168.34 eV) with an area ratio of 2:1 reflected the presence of C-SO3 on the periphery of the CPDs-3 [27]. The other two peaks of S 2p3/2 (169.28 eV) and S 2p1/2 (170.48 eV) with an area ratio of 2:1 revealed the presence of C-SO4 on the periphery [27].

The contents of C, N, O, S elements in the six CPDs were calculated and given in Fig. S3 and Table S1. The S element only came from the CR structure and the highest content of S element in the CPDs-3 (5.00%) demonstrated the highest content of CR on the periphery of the CPDs-3. At the same molar ratio of Trp/CR, the CR content on the periphery firstly enhanced and then decreased with the increasing dosage of 1,3-propylenediamine. This demonstrated that the moderate increase of 1,3-propylenediamine favored the reaction of CR into the CPDs structure but the redundant 1,3-propylenediamine intervened the reaction of CR. At the same dosage of 1,3-propylenediamine (1-mL), the S content of the CPDs-3 was more than that of the CPDs-4, which indicated the higher content of CR in the CPDs-3 than that in the CPDs-4. The result showed that the CPDs-3 may possess better potential amyloid-fibrillation-resistant ability through interacting with β-sheet structure of protein.

FT-IR measurement (Fig. 1I) was further performed to study the characteristic groups on the periphery of the CPDs-3. The stretching vibration bands of N-H/O-H, C=O, C=C, C-N, S-O, S=O, C-O, C-S in CPDs-3 were observed at 3670-3032 cm-1, 1600 cm-1, 1510 cm-1, 1370 cm-1, 1170 cm-1, 1120 cm-1, 1040 cm-1, 623 cm-1, respectively [26,27,30]. This demonstrated that there were -COOH, -NH2, -CONH, -C6H5 and sulfonic acid groups on the periphery of the CPDs-3. Comparing the FT-IR of the CPDs-3 with that of CR and tryptophan, it could be concluded that both CR and tryptophan participated in the formation process of the CPDs-3. This result was consistent with that of XPS.

The thermal decomposition process of the CPDs-3 (Fig. 2 A) was similar to that of traditional polymers, which revealed the presence of polymer chain in the CPDs-3 structure. At the temperature from 25 °C to 270 °C, the weight loss was mainly attributed to the remove of crystal water on the periphery of the CPDs-3. There were three degradation peaks in the DTG curve from 270 °C to 446 °C, attributing to the decomposition of functional groups, the fracture of polymer chain on the shell and the thermal decomposition of polymer network in the inner structure, respectively [31,37,44]. The weight loss at the temperature range of 446–700 °C described the pyrolysis of active fractions in the carbon core. The residue weigh was 49.3% above the temperature 700 °C, which suggested the presence of the graphene structure in the carbon core. The glass transition temperature (Tg) was further measured by DCS to confirm the presence of polymer chains (Fig. 2B). The relatively high Tg of 103.03 °C for the CPDs-3 indicated that the CPDs-3 possessed the more rigid polymer chains [45].

In addition, the 1H NMR was used to characterize the structure of the CPDs-3. As shown in Fig. 2C, the chemical shift at 6-10 ppm of the CPDs-3 was the same as that of CR (Fig. 2D), but the peaks of tryptophan did not appear in the 1H NMR of the CPDs-3. Comprehensive conclusion of the above analysis, it was inferred that the CPDs-3 was the classic core/shell structure and the shell was mainly composed of polymer chain, NH2 groups and CR structure.

The spectral properties of the CPDs-3 were studied by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence measurement. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CPDs-3 was similar to that of CR and there were three peaks at 236 nm, 346 nm and 498 nm (Fig. 2E). The absorption peak at 236 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C on the core of CPDs-3 [27]. The absorption peak at 346 nm belonged to the n-π* transition of C = O and C = N [26]. The peak at 498 nm was the absorption of the long conjugate structure in CR moiety [26]. The results demonstrated the severe similarity between CPDs-3 and CR, which was in line with the results of 1H NMR. There were two fluorescence excitation peaks at 285 nm and 410 nm in the fluorescence excitation spectra of the CPDs-3 (Fig. 2F), which was attributed to the π-π* transition of carbon core and n-π* transition of the groups on the periphery [24], respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra of the CPDs-3 were independent of excitation wavelength. The maximum fluorescence emission wavelength was found locating at 632 nm with the excitation wavelength of 410 nm. This indicated that the CPDs-3 possessed tremendous potential application in biological imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CPDs on HL amyloid fibrillation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A was the inhibition ratio (IR) of reactant of CR and tryptophan and the CPDs on HL amyloid aggregation after incubating for 5 days at 60 °C. The reactants of CR and tryptophan only showed weak inhibition ability on HL amyloid aggregation and their IR were 58.2% for CR and 21.7% for tryptophan, respectively. Yet, The prepared six CPDs showed higher inhibition ratio than that of the reactants. Comparing the CPDs-1 with the CPDs-2, the dosage of 1,3-propylenediamine was the same and the CR dosage of the CPDs-2 was more than that of the CPDs-1 (Table S1). The inhibition ratio of the CPDs-2 (90.5%) was greater than that of the CPDs-1 (77.9%), which demonstrated that the inhibition ability of the CPDs on HL amyloid aggregation was enhanced with the increasing of CR dosage. Comparing the CPDs-1 and the CPDs-3, the molar ratio of Trp/CR was the same and the dosage of 1,3-propylenediamine for the CPDs-3 was more than that of the CPDs-1. The inhibition ratio of the CPDs-3 (93.9%) was greater than that of the CPDs-1 (77.9%). Yet, the further increasing of 1,3-propylenediamine dosage induced the decreasing of IR for the CPDs-5 and the CPDs-6. Therefore, among these six CPDs, the CPDs-3 showed the highest IR on HL amyloid aggregation. From the XPS analysis, the highest S element content in the CPDs-3 indicated the highest CR content. This result affirmed that the CR unit in the CPDs-3 played the critical role in the inhibition process on amyloid aggregation.

In order to further explore the significance of Congo red on CPDs surface on the inhibition of HL amyloid aggregation, the CR-CPDs was synthesized using Cong red as the sole carbon source under the same reaction condition with that of the CPDs-3. The IR of the CR-CPDs on HL amyloid fibrillation at different concentrations were also studied by ThT fluorescence measurement (Fig. S4A). From Table S2, the IR of CR-CPDs was lower than that of the CPDs-3 under the same concentration. The IC50 of the CR-CPDs (8.38 μg/mL) was higher than that of the CPDs-3 (0.41 μg/mL) (Fig. S4B and Table S2). These results demonstrated that the inhibition capability of the CR-CPDs was weaker than that of the CPDs-3. For discussing the reason, XPS spectrum of the CR-CPDs was measured and given in Fig. S4C. The lower content of S element in the CR-CPDs (2.14%) compared with the CPDs-3 (5.00%) indicated the less CR on the surface of the CR-CPDs (Table S3), which may be the main reason of the weaker inhibition capability of the CR-CPDs on HL amyloid fibrillation. Therefore, addition of the moderate ratio of Trp could favor the increasing of inhibition capability of the prepared CPDs on HL amyloid aggregation.

The binding ability of the prepared CPDs with β-sheet structure could be investigated by monitoring the change of the fluorescence intensity. The obtained results were given in Fig. 3B. The value of (Ff−F0)/(Fm−F0) was correlated to the binding ability and the higher value reflected the stronger binding interaction with β-sheet structure. F0, Ff, Fm, were the value of fluorescence intensity for the CPDs samples, the CPDs samples mixing with amyloid fibrils and the model protein, respectively. CR also showed the binding ability with β-sheet structure and the same result was also reported in previous literature [30]. In these six CPDs, the value of the CPDs-3 was the highest, which indicated that the CPDs-3 possessed the strongest binding ability with β-sheet structure. The binding interaction between CPDs and protein further retarded protein aggregation into amyloid fibrils. Although the binding ability of the other five CPDs was weaker than that of CR, they still showed better inhibition ability on amyloid aggregation than CR. This could be interpreted by the more binding sites on the periphery of the CPDs comparing with the single CR molecule. In addition, the presence of conjugated-graphene carbon core further increased the interaction with aromatic amino acid residue through π-π and hydrophobic interaction [46,47].

TEM was further utilized to verify the interaction between the CPDs-3 and amyloid fibrils. For mature HL amyloid fibrils, their surfaces were smooth and clean (Fig. 3C). Some of the CPDs-3 were absorbed onto the amyloid fibrils after co-incubating for 15 min (Fig. 3D), which demonstrated the CPDs-3 could interact with HL fibrils by combining with β-sheet structure.

ThT fluorescence intensity was greatly increased after it was bound with the β-sheet structure of amyloid fibrils, therefore ThT was usually utilized as indicator to probe the presence of amyloid fibrils. Herein, HL was used as model protein to study the effect of the CPDs-3 on amyloid aggregation and the ThT kinetics curves were shown in Fig. 4 A. For native HL, the formation process of amyloid fibrils could be divided into three stages (lag phase stage, rapid fibrillation growth stage and final plateau stage) [48,49]. After the CPDs-3 was added into the incubated HL samples, the ThT fluorescence intensity obviously decreased comparing with the samples without the CPDs-3. This result demonstrated that the CPDs-3 could inhibit the formation of amyloid fibrils and the IR was enhanced with the increasing of the concentration. The maximum IR could reach 95.8% when the concentration was 40 μg/mL (Fig. 4B). The IC50 value was obtained by fitting the IR of different concentration and the value was 0.41 μg/mL (Fig. 4C).

TEM was used to observe the effect of the CPDs-3 on the morphology of amyloid aggregation (Fig. 5 A and 5B). When native HL was incubated at 60 °C for 5 days, plenty of long amyloid fibrils were formed. However, no long amyloid fibrils were found after the co-incubation of HL with 40 μg/mL the CPDs-3, which demonstrated that the CPDs-3 could completely inhibit the formation of HL amyloid fibrils at this concentration. This result was consistent with that of ThT fluorescence measurement.

As was shown in Fig. 5C, there were two negative peaks at 209 nm and 223 nm for native HL, which represented α-helix structure [50,13]. The calculated α-helix content was 25.4% for native HL. After culturing for 5 days, negative peak of 217 nm representing β-sheet structure appeared in HL aggregates [13]. The α-helix content was decreased from 25.4% to 7.1% and the β-sheet content was increased from 20.6% to 34% (Fig. 5D), which demonstrated that large quantity of mature fibrils were formed. When the CPDs-3 of 0.5 μg/mL was added to HL incubated samples, the peak at 217 nm was obviously decreased comparing with HL aggregates. The content of β-sheet structure was also decreased from 34% to 27.5%. When the dosage of the CPDs-3 was increased to 40 μg/mL, the CD spectrum was every similar to that of the native HL. The content of β-sheet structure (22.2%) was close to that of native HL (20.6%), which indicated that the addition of the CPDs-3 could stabilize the secondary structure of HL and further intervene the HL aggregation.

The best inhibition ability of the CPDs-3 on HL aggregation was related to its structure. Basing on the structure characterization, the CPDs-3 was a classical core-shell structure and the shell was mainly composed of CR molecular unit. The presence of CR in the shell and the carbon core with graphene structure endowed the CPDs-3 capability to interact with the β-sheet structure in HL through π-π stacking or hydrophobic interactions [33,47,48]. The binding interaction could maintain the natural conformation of protein and interven the amyloid aggregation of HL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytotoxicity and the cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of CPDs-3 on bEnd.3 cell was evaluated by CCK-8 experiment. As disclosed in Fig. 6 A, the CPDs-3 showed nontoxicity to bEnd.3 with a cell viability of 97% at 500 μg/mL. The imaging of the CPDs-3 in cell bEnd.3 was observed using laser confocal microscope. In the bright image (Fig. 6B), spindle-shaped cells could be observed, which demonstrated that the cells were in a good state of survival. Fig. 6C was the image observed within the bands of 600-650 nm. It could be clearly found that the CPDs-3 could enter into the cell interior (Fig. 6D) after the CPDs-3 were co-incubated with bEnd.3 for 4 h. The bEnd.3 was a kind of endothelial cell and the endothelial cell combined with microglia to generate the blood brain barrier [51,52]. The penetrating capability of the CPDs-3 to bEnd.3 cell suggested its potential application in treatment for Alzheimer's disease.

Oxidative stress could be induced by the generated toxic aggregates in the procedure of amyloid aggregation, which could further deteriorate the corresponding disease [53]. Therefore, the capturing of free radical became another effective method to treat the amyloid-related disease. Herein, three free radicals (DPPH•, •OH and O2 •-) were used to probe the capturing capability of reactants (CR and tryptophan) and the prepared CPDs. For DPPH•, the UV–Vis absorption peak at 517 nm was decreased when the six CPDs were added, which revealed that all of these six CPDs could eliminate DPPH• and the CPDs-4 showed the highest elimination efficiency (Fig. 7 A, D). For •OH, the CPDs-4 also showed the highest elimination efficiency (Fig. 7B, E). From Table S1, the content of N element in the CPDs-4 was the highest (13.4%) in these six CPDs, which implied that the content of NH2 groups on the CPDs-4 was the most. The highest elimination efficiency of the CPDs-4 may be caused by the strong reducibility of NH2 groups [54]. For O2 •-, the CPDs-6 showed a higher elimination efficiency comparing with the reactants of CR and Trp and the other five CPDs (Fig. 7C, F).

The elimination efficiency of the CPDs-3 was located in the middle, but the CPDs-3 showed the highest IR on HL aggregation. Therefore, the elimination efficiency of the CPDs-3 with different concentration was further investigated (Fig. S5, 7G, 7H and 7I). At 120 µg/mL, the elimination efficiency of the CPDs-3 for DPPH• reached 87.9%. And at 80 µg/mL, the elimination efficiency of the CPDs-3 for •OH reached 98.6%. The EC50 value of the CPDs-3 for DPPH• and •OH were <60 μg/mL and <45 μg/mL, respectively. But the elimination efficiency for O2 •- was only 35.1% when the concentration of the CPDs-3 was 200 μg/mL. It could be concluded that the EC50 value for O2 •- was higher than 200 μg/mL. When the dosage of the CPDs-3 was above 200 μg/mL, the absorbance could not be accurately measured. Therefore, the EC50 value of the CPDs-3 for O2 •- could not be given. In summary, the CPDs-3 showed the best scavenging ability for •OH at the same concentration.

In this study, six CPDs were synthesized by changing the molar ratio of CR and tryptophan through simple one-step method in microwave oven. All of these six CPDs could retard the HL amyloid aggregation, yet their inhibition capabilities were greatly different. The CPDs-3 with more CR unit on the surface displayed the highest IR on amyloid aggregation, which revealed that CR unit played a crucial role in the inhibition effect. In particular, when the inhibition ratio was 95.8%, the required concentration of the CPDs-3 (40 μg/mL) was lower than that in the literature [30]. The enhanced inhibition capability of the CPDs-3 was attributed to the introduction of tryptophan. The synergistic effect of CR unit and tryptophan in the CPDs-3 made it display a better inhibition capability on protein amyloid aggregation. In addition, the CPDs-3 also could be applied in cell imaging due to its red-emissive property as well as capturing abundant free radical. In summary, this investigation not only developed a series of new CPDs, but also opened a practical application for multifunctional CPDs in the treatment of the amyloid-related disease.

Jiawei Dong: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Dexin Li: Software, Methodology. Xinnan Wang: Formal analysis. Gang Ma: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xinwu Ba: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.
